cards.back: Wax/foreign body · otitis media or OME (glue ear) · TM perforation · otosclerosis (stapes fixation) · cholesteatoma/ossicular erosion. Weber → bad ear; Rinne negative.
Presbycusis · noise/acoustic trauma · ototoxic drugs · Ménière's · acoustic neuroma (+ congenital, infective, sudden SNHL). Weber → good ear; Rinne positive.

cards.front: Which drugs are ototoxic? (and which is NOT)
cards.back: A CLaSs of Quiet: Aminoglycosides · Cisplatin · Loop diuretics · Salicylates · Quinine. NOT ototoxic: penicillin/amoxicillin (classic trick).

Failure of the Eustachian tube to equalise middle-ear pressure during rapid ambient pressure change (diving/flying), esp. with a blocked ET (URTI). → TM retraction, congestion, effusion, ± perforation. Pain, fullness, conductive loss.

cards.front: Bacteria causing otitis externa + main treatment
cards.back: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (commonest) + Staph aureus. Treatment = aural toilet + topical antibiotic/steroid drops, keep dry, analgesia. Pain on pulling pinna / tragal tenderness.

cards.front: How is the tympanic membrane repaired?
cards.back: Myringoplasty (TM only) / tympanoplasty (TM ± ossicles). Graft = temporalis fascia (or tragal/conchal cartilage perichondrium).

Keratinising squamous epithelium trapped in the middle ear, eroding bone. Scanty foul otorrhea + attic/marginal perforation with white debris. "Unsafe" CSOM → facial palsy, vertigo, intracranial spread. Surgical treatment.
Strep pneumoniae · H. influenzae · Moraxella catarrhalis · Strep pyogenes · Staph aureus (viral URTI precedes). Bulging red TM, otalgia, fever.

Intratemporal: mastoiditis, facial palsy, labyrinthitis, Bezold/petrositis. Intracranial: meningitis, brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis. TRAP: cavernous sinus thrombosis is NOT OM — it's sinusitis.
In the external auditory canal ("surfer's ear") — from repeated cold-water exposure. Multiple, bilateral, broad-based bony swellings.

Middle ear → nasopharynx. ~36 mm; posterolateral 1/3 bony, anteromedial 2/3 cartilaginous. Opened by tensor veli palatini (swallow/yawn). Functions: ventilation, protection, drainage. Child's = short/wide/horizontal → more OM.
Auriculotemporal (V3) · great auricular (C2/3) · lesser occipital (C2) · Arnold's — auricular branch of vagus (X) · small VII & IX. Why ear syringing can trigger cough (X).

cards.front: Cochlea — what does the basilar membrane separate?
cards.back: Three scalae: vestibuli (perilymph) – media (endolymph) – tympani (perilymph). Basilar membrane = between scala media & scala tympani, carries the organ of Corti. Reissner's membrane = media/vestibuli.
Episodic vertigo (20 min–hrs) + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Cause = endolymphatic hydrops.

cards.front: How long does BPPV last + test?
cards.back: Vertigo lasts seconds (<30 s–1 min) triggered by head-position change. Otoconia in (usually posterior) semicircular canal. Dix-Hallpike diagnoses, Epley treats. No hearing loss.
Vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + nystagmus (toward healthy side) ± nausea/vomiting. Vestibular neuritis = identical but NO hearing loss.
Peripheral: hearing loss often present, nystagmus suppressed by fixation, no neuro signs. Central: no hearing loss, vertical/changing nystagmus, + neuro signs (diplopia, ataxia).

cards.front: What is NOT used to assess peripheral vestibular symptoms?
cards.back: Used: Dix-Hallpike, caloric test, head-impulse, Romberg/Unterberger, VNG/ENG, Frenzel glasses. MRI is for central causes — not the peripheral vestibular work-up.
ICA + ECA anastomose at Little's area (Kiesselbach). ECA: sphenopalatine (artery of epistaxis) + greater palatine + superior labial. ICA: anterior + posterior ethmoidal arteries.
Smell = CN I. General = trigeminal: V1 (ant/post ethmoidal) + V2 (nasopalatine + lateral nasal branches via pterygopalatine ganglion). Secretomotor via greater petrosal → Vidian.

cards.front: Lateral nasal wall drainage (which sinus → meatus)
cards.back: Inferior = nasolacrimal duct · Middle = frontal + maxillary + ant/mid ethmoid · Superior = posterior ethmoid · Sphenoethmoidal recess = sphenoid.

cards.front: Which sinus drains to the SUPERIOR meatus?
cards.back: Posterior ethmoid cells. (Sphenoid drains higher still, into the sphenoethmoidal recess.)
ICA + optic nerve (lateral, may be dehiscent) · pituitary/sella turcica (superior — trans-sphenoidal route) · cavernous sinus · Vidian + maxillary nerves. Drains to sphenoethmoidal recess.
Roof = floor of orbit (infraorbital n.). Floor = alveolus / molar roots (dental sinusitis, oroantral fistula). Medial = lateral nasal wall (ostium high → poor drainage). Largest sinus.

cards.front: Bacterial rhinosinusitis — signs & what to prescribe
cards.back: Facial pain/pressure (worse leaning forward), purulent discharge, nasal block, hyposmia, fever, tooth pain. Prescribe: antibiotic (amox±clav) + decongestant ± intranasal steroid.

Orbital (commonest, ethmoid): preseptal → orbital cellulitis → subperiosteal/orbital abscess → cavernous sinus thrombosis. Intracranial: meningitis, abscess. Bony: Pott's puffy tumour (frontal osteomyelitis).

cards.front: Cardinal symptom of nasal polyps + key associations
cards.back: Nasal obstruction (+ anosmia, rhinorrhea). Pale, insensitive, mobile, in middle meatus. Assoc: CRS, asthma, aspirin (Samter's triad), cystic fibrosis (in a child), allergic fungal.

Deviated septum · foreign body (child + foul discharge) · antrochoanal polyp · choanal atresia · tumour (inverted papilloma, juvenile angiofibroma in teen boy, sinonasal/NPC) · septal haematoma. ⚠️ Unilateral + bloody = exclude tumour.

Sneezing, watery rhinorrhea, nasal itch, congestion, itchy/watery eyes, postnasal drip, allergic salute, allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines, transverse nasal crease, pale boggy turbinates, cobblestone pharynx, hyposmia.
Trauma/nose-picking · foreign body · infection (rhinitis/sinusitis) · deviated septum/spurs · tumour (JNA, NPC) · dry air. 90% at Little's area.
Hypertension · anticoagulants/antiplatelets · bleeding disorders (haemophilia, ↓platelets, leukaemia) · liver/renal failure · HHT (Osler-Weber-Rendu) · vit K/C deficiency.

cards.front: Where does nasopharyngeal carcinoma arise from?
cards.back: The fossa of Rosenmüller (lateral pharyngeal recess, behind the ET cushion). EBV-related, Southern Chinese, salted fish.
Neck node (commonest sign) · unilateral adult OME (ET block — red flag!) · bloody nasal discharge/obstruction · cranial nerve palsies (V, VI, Horner's).

cards.front: In an adult with OME — what do you exclude?
cards.back: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma obstructing the Eustachian tube orifice at the fossa of Rosenmüller. → Scope the nasopharynx.

Severe unilateral sore throat + odynophagia · trismus · "hot potato" muffled voice · drooling · uvula deviated to opposite side · bulging soft palate · fever. NOT a symptom: bilateral symmetrical swelling.
Viral (commonest): rhino/adeno/EBV. Bacterial: Group A Strep (S. pyogenes), H. influenzae, Staph aureus, Strep pneumoniae, anaerobes (Vincent's), C. diphtheriae.
Suppurative: quinsy, parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal abscess, otitis media, Lemierre. Non-suppurative (post-strep): rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis, scarlet fever.

Recurrent tonsillitis (Paradise ≥7/yr…) · obstructive sleep apnoea · recurrent quinsy · suspected malignancy/unilateral enlargement · febrile convulsions. NOT: a single episode / small asymptomatic tonsils.
EBV. Fever + membranous tonsillitis + posterior cervical nodes + splenomegaly. Atypical lymphocytes, Monospot. Ampicillin → rash; avoid contact sport (splenic rupture).
Nasal block → mouth breathing + hyponasal voice + snoring · ET block → OME / recurrent AOM / conductive loss · adenoid facies · OSA / failure to thrive.
Glottic: sparse lymphatics → early hoarseness, good prognosis. Supraglottic: rich lymphatics → early bilateral nodes, presents late (throat pain, dysphagia, referred otalgia), poor prognosis.
RLN palsy (thyroid/lung/oesophageal Ca, aortic aneurysm) · neurological (MG, bulbar) · hypothyroidism · GERD/LPR · functional/vocal abuse. >3 wk in a smoker → scope.

cards.front: Causes of stridor in neonates (& "which is NOT")
cards.back: Laryngomalacia (commonest, omega epiglottis, worse supine, resolves by 2 y) · vocal cord palsy · subglottic stenosis/haemangioma · vascular ring. Croup/epiglottitis are older children, not neonates.
Epiglottitis (H. flu B) · croup (parainfluenza) · laryngeal oedema/anaphylaxis · bilateral RLN palsy · laryngeal tumour · foreign body · deep neck abscess.
Pharyngitis/quinsy/abscess · foreign body · oesophageal/postcricoid carcinoma · neurological (stroke, MG, bulbar) · achalasia/stricture · extrinsic (goitre, mediastinal).

Rapid bilateral cellulitis of submandibular/sublingual/submental spaces, usually odontogenic. Board-like floor of mouth, tongue pushed up, trismus, drooling, airway obstruction. Airway first → IV antibiotics → drainage.

cards.front: Which structure separates the vocal cords?
cards.back: The rima glottidis (the glottis) — the aperture between the true cords. (The laryngeal ventricle separates true from false cords.)
Nasopharynx (above soft palate): adenoids, ET openings + torus tubarius, fossa of Rosenmüller. Oropharynx (soft palate→epiglottis): palatine + lingual tonsils, base of tongue, Waldeyer's ring.

cards.front: Taste sensation of the anterior 2/3 of tongue
cards.back: Chorda tympani (branch of facial nerve VII), travelling with the lingual nerve. (General sensation = lingual/V3.)
Lingual artery (1st/2nd branch of the external carotid) → dorsal lingual, deep lingual, sublingual branches. Tonsillar branch of facial + ascending pharyngeal supply the root.

cards.front: Where is cancer most frequent in the oral cavity?
cards.back: Floor of mouth and lateral/ventral border of the tongue (SCC). Risk: tobacco, alcohol, betel, HPV; preceded by leukoplakia/erythroplakia.

cards.front: Overgrowth of the filiform papillae is called?
cards.back: Hairy tongue (lingua villosa) — elongated keratinised filiform papillae (black if pigmented). Poor hygiene, smoking, antibiotics, ↓saliva. Benign.
A white patch that can't be rubbed off & isn't another disease — a clinical descriptive term, premalignant → biopsy. ~5% → SCC. Erythroplakia is worse. Tobacco/alcohol/HPV/irritation.
Stensen's (parotid) duct → opens opposite the 2nd upper molar. The facial nerve (VII) runs through the gland.
Autoimmune exocrinopathy → dry eyes + dry mouth (sicca) + bilateral parotid swelling. Anti-Ro (SSA)/anti-La (SSB). Schirmer/lip biopsy. ↑↑ risk of B-cell (MALT) lymphoma — key point.

cards.front: Which salivary gland tumour is bilateral?
cards.back: Warthin's tumour (papillary cystadenoma lymphomatosum) — older male smokers, can be bilateral/multifocal. (Pleomorphic adenoma = commonest overall but unilateral.)

cards.front: Which parotid swelling is NOT painful?
cards.back: Pleomorphic adenoma (benign, slow, painless). Pain/rapid growth/VII palsy point to malignancy (e.g. adenoid cystic, mucoepidermoid).

cards.front: Signs of malignancy in a parotid tumour (5)
cards.back: Facial nerve palsy (most reliable) · pain · rapid growth · hard/fixed mass or skin ulceration · cervical nodes · trismus. (Benign tumours don't invade VII.)
Stone (sialolithiasis) · mumps (bilateral) · bacterial sialadenitis · Sjögren's · sarcoidosis · tumour · sialadenosis (diabetes, alcohol, bulimia). "Which does NOT" → something unrelated.
Submandibular ~80%: uphill duct + mucinous, Ca-rich secretion. Risk: stasis/dehydration, duct anatomy, high mucin/calcium, infection, drugs (anticholinergics). Mealtime swelling/pain.
Infective (EBV mono, HIV, TB, cat-scratch, toxoplasma) · malignant (lymphoma, leukaemia, metastatic SCC) · autoimmune (SLE, RA, Sjögren's, sarcoid) · drugs (phenytoin) · misc (Kawasaki, Kikuchi).
Hard, fixed, painless, >2 cm, supraclavicular, persistent >6 wk, + B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, >10% weight loss), hepatosplenomegaly. Age >40.

cards.front: Lateral neck mass in adult >40 — rule?
cards.back: Metastatic SCC until proven otherwise (often cystic, from HPV oropharynx/NPC). → image + FNA + panendoscopy; never excise first.

cards.front: What is NOT possible/likely for a neck lump under 18?
cards.back: Metastatic carcinoma — rare in this age. Under 18, think congenital (thyroglossal, branchial, cystic hygroma) or inflammatory (reactive nodes), or lymphoma.
Midline; moves up on swallowing AND on tongue protrusion (tract to foramen cecum via hyoid). Commonest congenital midline neck mass.

cards.front: Clinical features of malignant thyroid tumour
cards.back: Solitary hard fixed nodule · rapid growth · cervical nodes · pressure features: stridor (trachea), dysphagia (oesophagus), hoarseness/cord palsy (RLN) · referred otalgia · mets.
I submental/submandibular · II–IV jugular chain (upper/mid/low) · V posterior triangle · VI central/paratracheal · VII superior mediastinum. Virchow = left supraclavicular (GI primary).

cards.front: Which neck lump is NOT contagious?
cards.back: Congenital cysts — thyroglossal & branchial cysts (and lipoma, carotid body tumour) are non-infective. Contagious ones involve transmissible organisms (TB nodes, mono, cat-scratch).
Bell's palsy (idiopathic, commonest) · Ramsay Hunt · otitis media/cholesteatoma · temporal bone fracture / parotid surgery · tumour (parotid Ca, acoustic neuroma) · stroke (UMN).
LMN = whole half of face including forehead (Bell's, Ramsay Hunt). UMN = forehead spared (stroke) — bilateral cortical supply to upper face.

cards.front: Ramsay Hunt — symptoms (& what is NOT present)
cards.back: LMN facial palsy + vesicles in concha/EAC + otalgia + SNHL/tinnitus + vertigo + taste loss. NOT present: forehead sparing (that's UMN) — Ramsay Hunt affects the whole face.

cards.front: Temporal bone fracture — longitudinal vs transverse
cards.back: Longitudinal (80%): lateral blow, TM perforation, conductive loss, CSF otorrhea. Transverse (20%): frontal blow, haemotympanum, SNHL + vertigo, higher facial palsy. Battle's sign, halo sign.
ABR/BERA (auditory brainstem response) — objective threshold; also OAE & tympanometry are objective. PTA, Weber, Rinne are subjective (need patient response).

cards.front: Otoscopy normal but ear pain — explain (5 nerves)
cards.back: Referred otalgia: V (teeth/TMJ/tongue) · IX (tonsil/oropharynx — post-tonsillectomy, oral Ca) · X (larynx/hypopharynx — supraglottic cancer!) · C2/C3 (cervical spine). Always hunt the source.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an indication for tonsillectomy?

options.C: A single uncomplicated episode of tonsillitis

questions.explanation: A single uncomplicated episode is not an indication. Recurrent infection, OSA, recurrent quinsy and suspected malignancy all are.

questions.stem: Which drug is NOT ototoxic?
questions.explanation: Amoxicillin/penicillin are not ototoxic. Aminoglycosides, cisplatin and loop diuretics (furosemide) are — "A CLaSs of Quiet".

questions.stem: Which neck lump is NOT contagious?
questions.explanation: A branchial cyst is congenital — no transmissible organism. The others are infective.

questions.stem: Which salivary gland tumour is usually bilateral?
questions.explanation: Warthin's tumour (older male smokers) is the one that can be bilateral/multifocal.

questions.stem: Which sinus drains into the superior meatus?
questions.explanation: Posterior ethmoid cells drain to the superior meatus. Frontal/maxillary/anterior ethmoid → middle; sphenoid → sphenoethmoidal recess.

questions.stem: Which nerve carries taste from the anterior 2/3 of the tongue?
questions.explanation: Chorda tympani (VII) = taste anterior 2/3. The lingual nerve carries general sensation there; IX does both for the posterior 1/3.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a symptom of peritonsillar abscess?

options.B: Uvula deviated to the opposite side

questions.explanation: Quinsy is unilateral — bulging on one side with the uvula pushed away. Symmetrical bilateral swelling is not typical.

questions.stem: Which organism most commonly causes otitis externa?
questions.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (+ Staph aureus). Also the organism of malignant otitis externa in diabetics.

questions.stem: Barotrauma of the ear is caused by:

options.B: Failure of the Eustachian tube to equalise middle-ear pressure

questions.explanation: Pressure change the ET cannot equalise → TM retraction, effusion, possible perforation. Worse with a blocked tube (URTI).

questions.stem: Which is NOT a feature of Ramsay Hunt syndrome?

options.A: Vesicles in the external auditory canal

questions.explanation: Ramsay Hunt is a LMN palsy → the whole half of the face is affected, so no forehead sparing (that's a central/UMN sign).

questions.stem: Which disease does NOT cause salivary gland inflammation/swelling?
questions.explanation: Otosclerosis is stapes fixation in the ear — nothing to do with salivary glands.

questions.stem: Which structure separates the (true) vocal cords?
questions.explanation: The rima glottidis (glottis) is the aperture between the true cords. The ventricle separates true from false cords.

questions.stem: The parotid gland drains into the mouth via a duct opening:

options.C: Under the tongue beside the frenulum

questions.explanation: Stensen's duct opens opposite the 2nd upper molar. (Wharton's/submandibular opens at the sublingual papilla.)

questions.stem: Squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity is most frequent at:
questions.explanation: Floor of mouth and lateral/ventral tongue are the highest-risk sites for oral SCC.

questions.stem: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma usually arises from:
questions.explanation: The fossa of Rosenmüller (lateral pharyngeal recess) — EBV-associated.

questions.stem: Overgrowth of the filiform papillae on the dorsum of the tongue is called:
questions.explanation: Hairy tongue (lingua villosa) — elongated keratinised filiform papillae; benign.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a typical primary site presenting with cervical nodal metastases?
questions.explanation: Head & neck SCC sites (nasopharynx, larynx, oral cavity) drain to neck nodes. A femoral (bone) primary does not present as a cervical node.

questions.stem: Which parotid swelling is typically NOT painful?
questions.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma is a benign, slow, painless lump. Pain suggests malignancy or infection.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a complication of middle-ear infection?
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis complicates sinusitis/facial infection. Middle-ear infection causes sigmoid sinus thrombosis, not cavernous.

questions.stem: In an adult with unilateral otitis media with effusion, you must exclude:
questions.explanation: Unilateral adult glue ear → exclude nasopharyngeal carcinoma obstructing the ET. Scope the nasopharynx.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a cause of stridor in neonates?
questions.explanation: Croup affects older infants/children (6 mo–3 y), not neonates. Laryngomalacia is the commonest neonatal cause.

questions.stem: Main treatment for otitis externa is:
questions.explanation: Aural toilet + topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops, keep the ear dry. Systemic antibiotics only if spreading.

questions.stem: Which test objectively measures the hearing threshold?
questions.explanation: ABR/BERA is objective. PTA, Weber and Rinne all need a patient response (subjective).

questions.stem: BPPV vertigo typically lasts:
questions.explanation: Seconds on head-position change. Ménière's lasts 20 min–hours; vestibular neuritis days.

questions.stem: The cardinal feature combination of Ménière's disease is:

options.B: Vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness

questions.explanation: Episodic vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness (endolymphatic hydrops).

questions.stem: The cardinal symptom of nasal polyps is:
questions.explanation: Nasal obstruction is the cardinal symptom (with hyposmia/rhinorrhea). Pale, insensitive, in the middle meatus.

questions.stem: Which is NOT used to evaluate peripheral vestibular function?
questions.explanation: MRI is for suspected central pathology. Dix-Hallpike, caloric and head-impulse test the peripheral vestibular system.

questions.stem: What is NOT possible/likely as the diagnosis of a neck lump in a patient under 18?
questions.explanation: Metastatic SCC is very unlikely under 18. Congenital cysts, reactive nodes and lymphoma dominate this age group.
